coredata.dc:title: Scalable and atom economic preparation of red-near-infrared emitted N-doped graphene quantum dots with a high quantum yield
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wei
Feng, Xiaoqiang
Zhao, Menghan
Wei, Zhiheng
Liu, Zhiduo
Wang, Gang
Guo, Qinglei
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: The development of an environmental-friendly and scalable preparation approach for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with high quantum yield is a focus area for GQDs. Hence, we describe an atom economic green chemistry strategy for nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs). The yield of N-GQDs is 98.5%. Moreover, due to the simple reaction and processes, N-GQDs can be synthesized on a large scale (the throughput of N-GQDs is 10 g). Besides, the intrinsic bandgap PL of N-GQDs can be adjusted by size modulation. The emission wavelength of N-GQDs is modulated from 405 nm to 698 nm with a high quantum yield (0.89–0.62). Furthermore, with the general nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo, the PL response-ability of the obtained GQDs can be further tuned. Different precursors with heteroatoms (such as S and Se) can be used for the preparation of dual- heteroatoms co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs). We also confirmed the utilization of the red-near-infrared emitted N-GQDs (emission wavelength: 698 nm) with high quantum yield (0.62) in bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Red-near-infrared emission
Environmental-friendly preparation
High quantum yield
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Utilization of N-GQDs-5 in fluorescent bio-imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The illustration for the synthesis process of N-GQDs. A real image of for the (b) N-GQDs aqueous solution, and (c) powder obtained via a large-scale preparation method. (d) TEM image of N-GQDs (inset: HR-TEM). (e) The size distribution histogram. (f) AFM image of N-GQDs, and (g) the corresponding height.

(a) MALDI-TOF MS spectrum of the indigo solution before (black) and after (red) the reaction. (b) Survey XPS spectrum of N-GQDs. (c) Deconvoluted high-resolution C-1 s spectrum of N-GQDs. (d) Deconvoluted high-resolution O-1 s spectrum of N-GQDs. (e) Deconvoluted high-resolution N-1 s spectrum of N-GQDs. (f) Schematic diagrams showing the nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo under the solvent-thermal environment.

(a) Uv-vis spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution. (b) Tauc plot (hν = αhνr, r = 1/2) of N-GQDs aqueous solution. (c) The radiative transition between defect energy levels and edge state energy levels. (d) PL and PLE spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution. The inset shows a real image of the N-GQDs aqueous solution under ultraviolet source.

(a) Normalized PL spectra of N-GQDs-1, N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-3, N-GQDs-4, and GQDs-5. (b) A real image of the N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-3, and N-GQDs-5 aqueous solution under ultraviolet source. (c) The PL intensity change of N-GQDs-5 aqueous solution under ultraviolet radiation. F and F0 are the PL intensities of N-GQDs-5 (0.2 mg/mL) at special time and t = 0, respectively. (d) PL intensity change of N-GQDs-5 (0.2 mg/mL) when diversified ions (0.1 M) were added. F and F0 are the PL intensities of N-GQDs-5 with and without ions, respectively. (e) Metabolic activity of Hela cells hatched with various concentrations of N-GQDs-5. Fluorescent microphotograph of Hela cells incubated with N-GQDs-5: (f) bright-field image, (g) dark-field (h) merge image.
A brief summary of GQDs preparation approaches.
A brief summary of the PL performance of some reported GQDs and N-GQDs obtained in this work.
Summary of the PL performance of N-GQDs obtained under different reaction conditions.
Summary of the doping concentration and PL performance of dual- heteroatoms co-doped GQDs.

head.ce:title: Scalable and atom economic preparation of red-near-infrared emitted N-doped graphene quantum dots with a high quantum yield

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics, Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics, Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100083, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of an environmental-friendly and scalable preparation approach for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with high quantum yield is a focus area for GQDs. Hence, we describe an atom economic green chemistry strategy for nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs). The yield of N-GQDs is 98.5%. Moreover, due to the simple reaction and processes, N-GQDs can be synthesized on a large scale (the throughput of N-GQDs is 10 g). Besides, the intrinsic bandgap PL of N-GQDs can be adjusted by size modulation. The emission wavelength of N-GQDs is modulated from 405 nm to 698 nm with a high quantum yield (0.89–0.62). Furthermore, with the general nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo, the PL response-ability of the obtained GQDs can be further tuned. Different precursors with heteroatoms (such as S and Se) can be used for the preparation of dual- heteroatoms co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs). We also confirmed the utilization of the red-near-infrared emitted N-GQDs (emission wavelength: 698 nm) with high quantum yield (0.62) in bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have excited the attention of researchers since their practical applications in biotechnology [1–3]. Now, one of the main topics is the development of adequate preparation approaches for GQDs with tunable photoluminescence (PL) performances (especially red-near-infrared emitted GQDs and high quantum yield) modulation [3–5]. Firstly, recent preparation approaches for GQDs mainly include the “top-down” [5] and “bottom-up” [6] strategies. Most “top-down” approaches rely on the decomposition or exfoliation of carbon materials (such as highly oriented pyrolytic graphite [7], carbon nanotubes [8], graphene [8] and C60 [9]) with chemical oxidation in strong oxidizing medium (such as the mixture of KClO3, KMnO4, H2SO4, and HNO3) [3]. With regards to the “bottom-up” approaches, which refer to the preparation of GQDs through carbonization/pyrolysis of small organic molecules or biomass materials [10]. Unfortunately, the low yield for GQDs of “top-down” or “bottom-up” approaches cannot meet the current requirement of GQDs large scale preparation. Moreover, the corrosive effluent (such as H2SO4 and HNO3) generated in the “top-down” approach [5] and the large-sized carbide generated in the “bottom-up” approach [6] results in environmental pollution during the preparation process of GQDs. Thus, the development of environmental-friendly and scalable preparation approaches is the primary problem of GQDs research.

Secondly, the preparation of red-near-infrared emitted GQDs with high quantum yield is concerned with the potential applications in fluorescence bioimaging [1] and photodynamic therapy [11]. However, the surface structures (surface functional groups) of most reported GQDs are highly complicated [12]. The PL process of these GQDs is attributable to the radiative transition between defect energy levels and edge state energy levels [13]. Thus, most GQDs show size-independent PL behavior [14]. Indeed, up to now, it is hard to obtain the red-near-infrared emitted GQDs [15]. One of the main problems is the preparation of GQDs, which show size-dependent intrinsic bandgap PL.

To solve the above two urgent problems, we report an effective environmental-friendly preparation approach for N-GQDs via the solvothermal treatment of indigo. Due to the nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo under solvothermal environment, the yield of N-GQDs is 98.5%. No by-products generated in the reaction, which indicates the suggested preparation approach is atom economic green chemistry reaction. With this simple reaction and processes, N-GQDs can be synthesized on a large scale. The throughput of N-GQDs is 10 g. More importantly, the intrinsic bandgap PL of N-GQDs can be adjusted by size modulation. And, the emission wavelength of N-GQDs is modulated in 405–698 nm with a high quantum yield (0.89–0.62) via controlling the reaction conditions. Moreover, with the general nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo, the PL response-ability of the obtained GQDs can be further tuned. Different precursors with heteroatoms (such as S and Se) can be used for the preparation of dual- heteroatoms co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs). Specially, we also confirmed the utilization of the red-near-infrared emitted N-GQDs (emission wavelength: 698 nm) with high quantum yield (0.62) in bio-imaging.

The preparation of N-GQDs was achieved through the solvothermal treatment of indigo. Typically, 26.2 mg (0.1 mmol) indigo was dissolved in 20 mL EtOH and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated up to 160 °C for 12 h. The reaction mixture was freeze-dried directly without further purification (Fig. 1a). The yield of N-GQDs is 98.5% by calculated from indigo, as shown in Fig. 1b, which is much higher than recently reported preparation approaches (Table 1 ). Noted that no by-products were generated in the reaction, which indicates the suggested preparation approach is atom economic green chemistry reaction. Moreover, due to the simple reaction and processes, N-GQDs can be synthesized on a large scale. Typically, 13 g indigo was dissolved in 8 L EtOH and transferred into a 10 L autoclave and heated up to 160 °C for 24 h. The throughput of N-GQDs is 10.5 g (Fig. 1c), the yield of N-GQDs in above large-scale preparation is 80.7% (calculated from indigo).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) were carried out for the characterization of the morphology of obtained N-GQDs. Fig. 1d displays the TEM image of N-GQDs, indicating the homogeneous dots with a lateral size of 1–5 nm presence in the scanned area. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image reveals the apparent crystal structure of N-GQDs, which indicates the crystal structure of them, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1d. The interlayer spacing (0.24 nm) corresponds to the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [16]. Fig. 1e shows that the radius size distribution histogram of N-GQDs with the average lateral size is 2.7 nm. The Contact mode AFM image of N-GQDs coated on the 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate is shown in Fig. 1f. A uniform height of N-GQDs is 0.5–1.2nm, indicating that 1–3 layers are formed (Fig. 1g) [4].

The MALDI-TOF MS was carried out to confirm the atom economy nature of the suggested preparation approach. The MALDI-TOF MS spectrum of indigo solution displays the bands at M/z = 263.2, as shown in Fig. 2a (black line), which can be attributed to the protonation of indigo. By contrast, after the reaction, the MALDI-TOF MS spectrum of indigo solution displays M/z = 1850–3100, as shown in Fig. 2a (red line), indicating 85–160 aromatic nuclei in a single N-GQDs [12] which matches the TEM results. Moreover, no signal assigned to indigo or oligomer can be observed. This indicates the obtained products are N-GQDs without by-products. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results clarified the surface chemical composition and chemical states of the obtained N-GQDs. Three peaks can be observed in the XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 2b). The peaks located around at 284 eV, 532 eV, and 399 eV correspond to C-1 s, O-1 s, and N-1 s peak, respectively [16]. The N content in N-GQDs is 10.8 at.% which close to the N content of indigo (10 at.%). This indicates the atom economic nature of the reaction. The C-1 s peak of N-GQDs can be split into three, i.e., the C-C/C=C at ~284.4 eV, and the C-O/C-N at ~285.9 eV, and the CO groups at ~287.7 eV [17], respectively, as shown in Fig. 2c. The O-1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d) implies the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups. The prominent peaks located around at 532.1 and 530.9 eV are attributed to C-OH and CO groups, respectively [4]. Moreover, numerous N atoms exist in the state of aromatic N (398.4 eV) and -NH2 (399.3 eV, Fig. 2e) [18]. XPS results demonstrated the definite chemical composition and chemical states of the obtained N-GQDs, which corresponds to the nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo under the solvent-thermal environment (Fig. 2f).

Uv-visible absorption (Uv-vis), photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were performed for the characterization of the optical features of N-GQDs. Fig. 3a depicts the Uv-vis spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution. The peak around at ~260 nm corresponds to π-π* transition of the sp
2 domains [19,20]. The optical bandgap (Eg) of N-GQDs is considered from the Tauc plot (hν = αhνr), where α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively. With regards to the direct bandgap material, r = 2 (Fig. 3b). Otherwise, r = 1/2 for an indirect band gap material [21]. A good linear fit when using r = 1/2, indicating that N-GQDs is an indirect band gap material, as shown in Fig. 3b. By measuring the x-axis intercept of an extrapolated line from the linear regime of the curve, the Eg value of N-GQDs was thus defined as 2.32 eV. It is well known that the surface structures (surface functional groups) of most reported GQDs are highly complicated [22,23]. The PL process of N-GQDs is caused by the radiative transition between defect energy levels and edge state energy levels (derived from surface functional groups, as shown in Fig. 3c) [24,25]. Thus, most GQDs show size-independent PL behavior [26,27]. Table 2 summarized the PL performance of some reported GQDs. Obviously, GQDs still emit blue-green PL even with large size. By contrast, the N-GQDs obtained via our approach show intrinsic bandgap PL. As shown in Fig. 3d, PL and PLE spectra of N-GQDs imply the optimal excitation wavelength at 534 nm and an emission wavelength at 598 nm, respectively. The excitation wavelength is corresponding to the Eg of N-GQDs. This indicates that the PL process can be due to the radiative transition between the intrinsic bandgap of N-GQDs. The quantum yield (φ) was measured according to established procedures [16]. Rhodamine 6G solution (φ = 0.98 in EtOH) was chosen as a standard [18]. To minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvettes were kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength. The φ of N-GQDs is approximately 0.76, which is much higher than reported recently from carbon dots, polymer dots, and GQDs [3,28–37]. Indeed, the N-GQDs aqueous solution exhibits bright red PL under ultraviolet irradiation (inset of Fig. 3d).

More importantly, the intrinsic bandgap PL of N-GQDs can be adjusted through size modulation. Different sized N-GQDs can be obtained via the modulation of reaction time. Table 3 and Fig. 4a present the PL properties of different sized N-GQDs. The emission wavelength of N-GQDs is modulated from 405 to 598 nm with high φ (0.89–0.62). The φ of N-GQDs-1, N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-3, N-GQDs-4, and N-GQDs-5 are 0.89, 0.84, 0.79, 0.73 and 0.62, respectively (Figs. 4a-b). The outstanding PL performance of near-infrared emitted N-GQDs (N-GQDs-5) suggests the important application value of N-GQDs-5.

In addition, N-GQDs present outstanding antijamming capability and stability. Fig. 4c displays the photostability of N-GQDs-5, less than 5% of the PL intensity was reduced under ultraviolet radiation for 72 h. Moreover, the PL intensity of N-GQDs-5 exhibits no noticeable change when diversified ions were added. The addition of Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Br−, and CO3 2− does not affect the PL property of N-GQDs-5, as shown in Fig. 4d.

Moreover, with the general nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo, the PL response-ability of the obtained GQDs can be further tuned. Different precursors with heteroatoms (such as S and Se) can be used for the preparation of dual- heteroatoms co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs). Thiophenol and selenophenol were selected as precursor for the preparation of N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs, respectively. Typically, 26.2 mg (0.1 mmol) indigo and 1.1 mg (0.01 mmol) thiophenol (or 1.5 mg (0.01 mmol) selenophenol) were dissolved in 20 mL EtOH and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated up to 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction mixture was freeze-dried directly without further purification. As shown in Table 4 . the S/Se doping concentration of N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs is 1.5 and 1.1 at.%, respectively. Meanwhile, the λem of N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs is 620 and 645 nm, respectively (also shown in Table 4). Noteworthily, due to the strong coordination tendency of S and Se, the PL of N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs can be quenched by Hg2+ and Cd2+, respectively. The detection limit of N, S-GQDs and N, Se-GQDs is 0.3 and 2.4 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of N-GQDs-5 in fluorescent bio-imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the N-GQDs-5 in fluorescent bio-imaging was studied. The Hela cell line was employed to test the in vitro cytotoxicity of N-GQDs-5. Fig. 4e shows the metabolic activity of Hela cells treated with different concentrations of N-GQDs-5 (0–500 μg/mL). The N-GQDs-5 were joined to the Hela cell cultured in 96 well-plates and incubated for 24 h. And, a standard assay was executed to estimate the Hela cell viabilities after the N-GQDs-5 addition. No noticeable reduction in cell viability was seen for Hela cells treated with N-GQDs-5 still under high concentrations (500 μg/mL), confirming that the N-GQDs-5 provided by our method was not toxic in vitro.

The N-GQDs-5 aqueous solution was utilized to incubate Hela cells to determine their bio-imaging ability measured by fluorescence microscopy, as shown in Figs. 4f-h. The deep red PL can be seen inside the cells (Fig. 4h), indicating that all N-GQDs-5 have been internalized by the Hela cells and act as a variety of valuable bio-imaging material.

In this work, we have developed an environmental-friendly and scalable preparation approach of red-near-infrared emitted N-GQDs with a high quantum yield. The N-GQDs were successfully prepared via the solvothermal treatment of indigo. No by-products generated in the reaction, which indicates the suggested preparation approach is atom economic green chemistry reaction. Due to the nucleophilic addition reaction of indigo under solvothermal environment, the yield of N-GQDs is 98.5%. With this simple reaction and processes, N-GQDs can be synthesized on a large scale (throughput is 10 g). More importantly, the intrinsic bandgap PL of N-GQDs can be adjusted by size modulation. The emission wavelength of N-GQDs is modulated from 405 nm to 698 nm with a high quantum yield (0.89–0.62) via controlling the reaction conditions. We also proved the utilization potentiality of the red-near-infrared emitted N-GQDs (emission wavelength: 698 nm) with high quantum yield (0.62).

Wei Zhu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Xiaoqiang Feng: Writing - review & editing. Menghan Zhao: Investigation. Zhiduo Liu: Investigation, Validation. Da Chen: Formal analysis. Qinglei Guo: Investigation. Zhiheng Wei: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Gang Wang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics under Grant (No. IOSKL2017KF06), Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2018A6100960), National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 11704204), and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. There are no conflicts of interest.
